result:
The spec is fully implemented. I added the three missing operations to `CsvRow.calr` in Calor, and the project builds cleanly with 0 warnings and 0 errors (2 build cycles used of the 10 budgeted).

What was added, alongside the untouched `NeedsQuoting`:

- **`EscapeField(field)`** — calls `NeedsQuoting`; when quoting is needed it doubles internal quotes with `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps the result in quotes, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow(fields)`** — iterates with `§EACH`, escaping each field and joining with commas via a mutable accumulator; an empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow(line)`** — a single-pass `§WH` scanner with an in-quotes flag: doubled quotes inside a quoted field emit one literal quote, commas/CR/LF inside quotes are content, an unquoted comma ends the field, and a final `§PUSH` emits the last field (so the empty line parses to one empty field and a trailing comma yields a trailing empty field). It declares the `mut` effect since it builds the list with `§PUSH`.

One wrinkle along the way: the exemplar's `[str]` type compiles to `string[]`, but the spec pins `List<string>` for `JoinRow`/`SplitRow`. I checked the compiler's type expansion and switched the signatures to `List<str>`, which generates exactly the pinned surface: `JoinRow(List<string>)` and `List<string> SplitRow(string)`.

I also verified behavior end-to-end from a throwaway console app referencing the built `Src.dll`: round-trip `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` holds across the tricky cases (embedded commas, doubled quotes, CR/LF content, empty/trailing fields, fields that are only quote characters), `JoinRow([])` is the empty string, and `SplitRow("")` returns a single empty field. The scratch app was deleted afterward; no test files were added to the project and the csproj is unmodified.
